figures.caption: Figure 2 | Scaling Goal Blocks and Tower Height. Gemini 3 Pro vs. LAMA-2011 and the theoretical optimal cost. (a) Increasing goal blocks: plan costs illustrating generalization to retrieving and stacking many goal blocks. (b) Increasing tower height: plan costs for retrieving and unstacking from high towers.

Table 1 | Grand Challenge: Detailed Results. Each row increases all complexity axes simultaneously. “–” indicates planner failure within 60s. For Gemini 3 Pro, ★ denotes an optimal valid plan matching 𝐶opt; × denotes failure to produce a valid plan. BW = Blocksworld; Graph = isomorphic graph representation.

Figure 1 | Structural Isomorphism between Blocksworld and 𝑃∗ Topology. (a) A standard Blocksworld instance illustrating the initial state (left) and the target goal state (right). The task requires rearranging blocks using four standard atomic actions: unstack, stack, pick-up, and put-down. (b) The corresponding 𝑃∗ graph representation of the initial state. The table serves as the central root node, and each stack of blocks forms a disjoint branch, framing the planning task as a structural path-traversal problem.

Table 2 | Harvest: Detailed Results. Fixed tower height 5–8, one goal block per tower (𝑆 =𝑊). “–” indicates planner failure within 60s. For Gemini 3 Pro, ★ denotes an optimal valid plan matching 𝐶opt; × denotes failure to produce a valid plan. BW = Blocksworld; Graph = isomorphic graph representation.

Table 3 | High Towers: Detailed Results. Fixed width 𝑊=12, one goal block (𝑆=1), tower height scaled from 8 to 993. “–” indicates planner failure within 60s. For Gemini 3 Pro, ★ denotes an optimal valid plan matching 𝐶opt; × denotes failure to produce a valid plan. BW = Blocksworld; Graph = isomorphic graph representation.
Figure 4 | Grand Challenge Example. The simplest problem from the Grand Challenge curriculum with initial state (left) and goal state (right).

Figure 3 | Scalability and Compute Allocation. (a) Grand Challenge: plan steps vs. total problem volume (ℎ × 𝑤 × 𝑠). Gemini 3 Pro tracks optimality into extreme complexities where classical search collapses. (b) Inference-time compute: thinking trace length (tokens) vs. optimal cost 𝐶𝑜𝑝𝑡. All curricula follow a linear trend (≈47 tokens/step); failed instances (gray crosses) cluster at higher token counts.

Table 4 | Interleaved Harvest Results. Towers with height 5–8, two goal blocks per tower. “–” indicates planner failure. For Gemini 3 Pro, ★ denotes an optimal valid plan matching 𝐶opt; ✓ denotes a valid but suboptimal plan confirmed by the VAL validator; × denotes failure to produce a valid plan.

Figure 5 | Interleaved Harvest Example (6 towers, 12 goal blocks). Initial state (left) with goal blocks highlighted in gold, and goal tower (right). The scrambled PDDL encoding and cross-tower dependencies make this problem significantly harder for classical planners than the single-goal Harvest.
